coredata.dc:title: TEMPO oxidized nanofiber carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composites with enhanced photocatalytic activity for degradation of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Jingwei
Guo, Yanzhu
Lu, Jie
Cheng, Yi
Wang, Haisong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots/titanium dioxide (CQDs/TiO2) nanocomposites were synthesized with TEMPO oxidized cellulose as carbon source via a facile method. In this eco-friendly system, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 were evaluated through photo-degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible light. The results show that CQDs/TiO2 composites possess high photocatalytic degradation efficiency up to 92%. The enhanced degradation efficiency of MB was probably attributed to that the photoelectrical properties of CQDs can lead to effective separation of photo-generated electrons-holes pairs. Moreover, the TEMPO oxidized cellulose has abundant amino groups, which can significantly improve the photophysical properties of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiber
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic activity
Methylene blue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs and nanohybrids of CQDs/TiO2
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs/TiO2 composites
Photocatalytic performance of the prepared CQDs/TiO2 composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Foundation Guidance Program of Liaoning Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21978029, 31770624), the Natural Foundation Guidance Program of Liaoning Province (No. 2019-ZD-0219, No. 1553069548324), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC2002024) and the Startup fund for Young Doctor at Dalian Polytechnic University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (a) CQDs and (b) CQDs/TiO2 (160–10).
SEM images of CQDs/TiO2 (160–10) (a) before and (b) after the CQDs/TiO2 composites cycle. (c) The element analysis of CQDs/TiO2 (160–10) by EDX.
XRD (a) and FTIR (b) patterns of the pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites samples CQDs/TiO2 (160–10), CQDs/TiO2 (160–20), and CQDs/TiO2 (160–60).
(a) UV–Vis absorption of CQDs, TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2(160–10) and (b) absorption study of CQDs/TiO2(160–10).
(a) Full scale XPS spectra of composites with a CQDs/TiO2 ratio of 1:1 for different heating times; (b) N1s spectra of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites with different heating time.
(a) Chemical structure of the MB dye and 1H NMR spectra for the (b) MB dye (i) before and (ii) after degradation.

(a) Effect of photocatalytic CQDs/TiO2 composites on degradation of MB under different condition; (b) Plot of (C/C0) vs time for photocatalytic degradation of different ratio of C/T; (c) Recycle study of the photocatalytic CQDs/TiO2.

(a) PL spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 (160–10) (excitation at 300 nm); (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 (160–10) composite; (c) ESR spectra of (a) DMPO-
OH (d) DMPO-O2 − in CQDs/TiO2 (160–10) photo-catalytic system under visible light irradiation.
Comparison between CQDs/TiO2 complex with other CQDs/Semiconductors.

head.ce:title: TEMPO oxidized nanofiber carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composites with enhanced photocatalytic activity for degradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Light Industry and Chemical Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Light Industry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Light Industry and Chemical Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Light Industry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots/titanium dioxide (CQDs/TiO2) nanocomposites were synthesized with TEMPO oxidized cellulose as carbon source via a facile method. In this eco-friendly system, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 were evaluated through photo-degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible light. The results show that CQDs/TiO2 composites possess high photocatalytic degradation efficiency up to 92%. The enhanced degradation efficiency of MB was probably attributed to that the photoelectrical properties of CQDs can lead to effective separation of photo-generated electrons-holes pairs. Moreover, the TEMPO oxidized cellulose has abundant amino groups, which can significantly improve the photophysical properties of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution has been considered one of the most severe environmental problems, and has caused widespread concern [1]. Especially, the wastewater discharged by the dye industry is a major pollution source that causes a lot of damage to the earth. In the past decades, photocatalyzed degradation has been considered as a promising method to treat wastewater due to its attractive properties, e.g., low-cost and green [2]. TiO2 remains as one of the most promising catalysts due to its high chemical stability properties, low cost, complete degradation and reutilization. However, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is restricted by many factors, such as its wide band gap (3.2 eV), the fast recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and the narrow absorption spectrum (only ultraviolet light), which therefore limited the practical applications of TiO2 [3]. To address those issues, various modification strategies have been developed, such as metal/non-metal elements doping, semiconductor coupling, and surface functionalization [4–6]. However, it is still challenging to find an industry-friendly method for preparing doped TiO2 with efficient photostability under visible light irritation in the respect of simplicity and accessibility. Surface property is considered as an important factor that notably influences photocatalytic activity of the TiO2-based catalysts. According to previous findings, introducing active polymers [7] and functional groups [8] onto the surface of TiO2 leads to significantly improved its photocatalytic performance. For example, surface hydroxyl groups not only enhance the water-dispersibility of TiO2 nanoparticles but also promote the generation of hydroxyl radicals for dye degradation. Meanwhile, the surface modification of TiO2 materials with amino groups can provide electron donors [9,10], and thus improve their photocatalytic performance”

Carbon quantum dots (CQD), which generally consists of a carbonaceous core surrounded by the oxygenated surface groups [11], have been reported to have the ability to improve the performances of photocatalysts [12]. CQDs are highly compatible with the aqueous chemistry and biomedical applications due to their low toxicity [13], environmental sustainability and good properties of photoexcited electron-hole pairs separation. Besides, modifying photocatalyst with ultra-small CQDs is promising to construct a full solar spectrum responsive photocatalyst owing to the size-dependent photoluminescence of CQDs [14]. Those advantages make CQDs especially one of the best options to pair with TiO2 [15] for high-efficient photodegradation of industrial pollutants [16]. Thakur et al. [17] presented studies on the design of an efficient tunable photocatalyst based on the composite of PM(Au,Ag)@CQD/TNF nanocomposites, which exhibits higher light absorption and higher photocatalytic activity as compared to pristine metal nanoparticles. Besides, Kang et al. have reported several CQDs/semiconductor composites for degrading organic pollutants in the aqueous or gas phase under the irradiation of visible light [18]. Nowadays, CQDs can be fabricated via a number of unconventional ways, and the simplest but efficient way is the carbonization/pyrolysis of the biomass materials [19,24] as the carbon sources. For example, CQDs were reported from the waste lemon peels [20,25], orange juice [21,26], plant extract [22,27], food waste [23,28] and so on. Those CQDs derived from natural biomaterials are eco-friendly, easy to prepare and usually abundant with oxygen-based functional groups.

Our hypothesis is that by introducing the TEMPO oxidized cellulose nanofiber as carbon source, the CQDs can be prepared and interacts with TiO2 to form surface functionalized CQDs/TiO2 composites with an improved photocatalytic degradation efficiency for the treatment of dye pollutants. The simple one-pot fabrication method in this work provides a practical route for the scale-up industrial synthesis of efficient photocatalysts. And more importantly, the TEMPO oxidized nanofibers used in this system makes it easy to separate the catalysts from the photodegraded MB water, which is beneficial for the reuse of the catalyst.

Methylene blue (MB), titanium dioxide (TiO2) with a particle size of 25 nm and urea were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Graphene oxide (GO) was obtained from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the solutions were prepared with deionized (DI) water. TEMPO oxidized cellulose nanofiber hydrogel (TOCNF, 1.08% wt) was donated by Tianjin University of Science and Technology (Tianjin, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and nanohybrids of CQDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, equal amount of TEMPO oxidized cellulose nanofiber hydrogel (TOCNF) and urea were ground in the mortar and were well mixed. The mixtures were added into the crucibles and heated at different temperatures (136 °C, 160 °C, 242 °C) for 15 mins in the electronic oven. After the product was cooling to room temperature, CQDs were obtained. The CQDs/TiO2 were prepared with the same procedure by adding different ratios of CQDs to TiO2 with a particle size of 25 nm (P25) in a mortar. After that, the samples were transferred into an electronic oven and maintained at a fixed temperature for different treating time (10 mins, 20 mins, 1 h and 2 h).

The photocatalytic activity tests were evaluated by photodegrading methylene blue (MB) solution under the irradiation of visible light. Typically, CQDs/TiO2 composites were dispersed in 40 mL MB solution (100 mg/L) in a quartz tube under ultrasonication at room temperature. Prior to photocatalysis, the resultant solutions were magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 mins to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The equilibrated solution then exposed to UV–visible light irradiation using 500 W Xe lamp with constant stirring. The equilibrated solution then exposed to UV–visible light irradiation with constant stirring. Samples were collected after every six minutes until the complete degradation of MB, which can be examined from the absorption spectra using UV–vis Llambda 750 spectrophotometer at 650 nm.

Samples were named as “CQDs/TiO2(x-y)” to indicate the parameters used in a typical synthesis, e.g., the heating temperature (x, °C) and the heating time (y, min). For example, CQDs/TiO2(160–10) refers to CQDs/TiO2 complex that is synthesized by heating the materials at 160 °C for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs/TiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained CQDs were studied by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the CQDs are spherical with a diameter of ∼3.0 nm, and the lattice spacing of around 0.285 nm is assigned to graphitic carbon. TiO2 was dispersed into CQDs by heating the CQD/TiO2 mixture at a certain temperature, so CQDs/TiO2 (160–10) were used for HRTEM characterization. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the interplanar spacing of 0.350 nm agrees well with the (1 0 1) plane of anatase TiO2 [29–31], and the average size of TiO2 is 7 nm. These results suggest the disperse of TiO2 in the composites. In addition, Fig. 2 shows the morphology of CQDs/TiO2 composites cycle using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites are composed of C, N, O, and Ti elements.

The XRD patterns of the CQDs/TiO2 samples were shown in Fig. 3 (a). The TiO2 (P25) that we used in the experiments possesses sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 25°, 38°, and 47°, corresponding to the crystal planes of (1 0 1), (0 0 4), and (2 0 0) of anatase TiO2. However, the CQDs that we synthesized in this work did not exhibit the characteristic diffraction peaks. There is no characteristic diffraction peaks of CQD, which could be attributed to the small amounts and amorphous state of CQDs in the composites (≤2.5 wt%).

FTIR technique was carried out to further illustrate the formation and surface functional groups of CQDs/TiO2 composites. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the peak assigned to stretching vibration of COH in the CQDs at 1400 cm−1 was shifted to 1445 cm−1 after heating together with TiO2, which could be another evidence for the formation of CQDs/TiO2 composites. The characteristic broad absorption band in the FTIR spectra of CQDs/TiO2 composites is from 3000 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1, suggesting the incorporation of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the composites. Two obvious peaks at about 1250 cm−1 and 1530 cm−1, were attributed to the skeletal vibration of CN and NH, indicating the existence of amide bonds on the surface of CQDs. In addition, the broad absorption at 1000 cm−1 showed a red shift which was associated with the combination of the TiOTi and TiOC vibrations.

In order to prove the functions of CQDs for improved excellent visible photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, UV–visible spectra and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurement were studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), pure TiO2 shows strong absorption in the UV region, and no absorb peaks in the visible light range. While CQDs/TiO2 displays obvious visible light absorption ranging from 400 to 800 nm. The results indicate that the CQDs/TiO2 composites could effectively broaden the absorption of TiO2 nanoparticles in the visible light region. Moreover, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of CQDs/TiO2 can be identified as type IV isotherm (IUPAC), indicating a mesoporous structure as shown in Fig. 4(b). According to the BET analysis, the specifc surface areas of the CQDs/TiO2 is measured to be 54.6 m2 g−1. The large surface area and porous structure could further suggest the degradation efficiency of organic pollutants on the surface of photocatalyst.

As shown in Fig. 5 (a), The XPS spectrum of the CQDs/TiO2 composites suggested the presence of titanium, carbon, and oxygen in the CQDs/TiO2 composites. Besides, the deconvoluted N(1s) XPS spectrum of the CQDs/TiO2 composites is provided in Fig. 5(b). The typical peaks at 398.6 and 400.8 eV are attributed to the sp2 aromatic CNC and amino group CNH, respectively. It is worth noting that, the contents of NH in the composites synthesized with a heating time of 10 min is the highest [9]. The full survey spectrum of Fig. 5(c) illustrated the presence of Ti 2p, C 1s and O 1s in the CQDs/TiO2 composites. The two Ti 2p peaks fitted at 458.8 and 464.5 eV are corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, respectively. The main peak of C 1s spectrum at 284.8 eV belongs to the CC bond of sp2 orbital. The peaks at 286.3 and 287.3 eV are attributed to the CO and CO bonds, respectively. The peaks of O 1s spectrum at 530.0, 530.7 and 532.1 eV are ascribed to TiO, CO and COH. Both the C 1s and O 1s spectra indicate that CO exists bonds in CQDs/TiO2 composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of the prepared CQDs/TiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: MB was chosen as a model dye to investigate the photodegradation performance of the CQDs/TiO2 composites. Fig. 6 (a) shows the chemical structure of MB molecule. 1H NMR was used to study the change of MB structure before and after the photodegradation process (samples were collected from supernatant pool of dye-photoactive-CD system), and the results are recorded in Fig. 6(b). 1H NMR peaks at 8.01 and 7.96 ppm are assigned to the aromatic protons. Those protons are significantly decreased after the photodegradation reaction, indicating the nearly complete ring-opening degradation of the thiazine structure, which is corresponding to the findings in previous publications [32,33]. Meanwhile, the intensity of the peaks representing the six aliphatic protons of MB from 2.84 to 3.43 ppm are also significantly decreased after the photodegradation, which is attributed to the degradation of methylene groups. These changes revealed by 1H NMR spectra verify the destroy of MB structure, suggesting the efficient photodegradation of MB is achieved by using the CQD/TiO2 catalyst.

As illustrated by the results of FTIR and XPS, changing the synthesis parameters such as heating temperature and heating time would yield CQD/TiO2 composites with different type and content of surface functional groups, e.g., amino groups, which may affect the photodegradation efficiency of CQD/TiO2 composites. To further investigate the catalytic properties of CQD/TiO2 composites, a series of CQD/TiO2 samples were synthesized under different heating temperature (136 °C, 160 °C, and 242 °C) and time (10 mins, 20 mins and 1 h). Then, the samples were used as catalysts for the photocatalytic degradation of MB. Pure CQDs and P25 were used as controls.

The initial concentration of MB was set to 100 mg/L. This relatively high concentration compared with other investigation papers [10] is aimed to better mimic the real wastewater. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the photocatalytic performance of TiO2/CQDs composites had a remarkable enhancement compared with pure CQDs and TiQ2. When the reaction time was fixed to 20 mins, the CQDs/TiO2 composites that were heated at 242 °C during the synthesis display a lower degradation efficiency of 62% than that of the composites heated at 160 °C (76%). The N(1s) XPS spectrum in Fig. 5(b) shows that the CQDs/TiO2 composites that were heated at 160 °C have the relative high amount of surface amino groups, which is probably because the high temperature breaks surface N-H bonds of the CQDs/TiO2 composites. We speculate that the amino groups may play a role in the photodegradation reaction since surface modification by amino groups has been reported to improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 mainly because the amino groups can serve as electron donor [34,35]. The electron-donating capacity of amino groups could serve as a hole trap to slow down the electron-hole recombination in the photocatalytic process [36]. Meanwhile, in aqueous solutions, MB exhibits an electron deficient aromatic structure with unstable CS± C structure, which easily transfers to CS(O)C structure either through being attacked by hydroxyl radicals or undergoing rearrangement facilitated by electron rich groups [37,38]. Then the generated CS(O)C could be further attacked by one more hydroxyl radical to form the first intermediate product. Thus, another possible mechanism for the amino groups mediated improvement of photodegradation could be that the electron rich amino groups on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 composites interact with the CS± C structure via coordination bond, and therefore promote the rearrangement to form the sulfoxide structure. In this way, the radicals required for the transition from CS± C to CS(O)C would reduce significantly. Thus, the photo-generated electrons could be used efficiently for the next degradation steps. In addition, the color removal efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 (136–20) is only 28% even after degradation reaction, which is significantly lower than the dye removal using the CQDs/TiO2(160–10) (92%). This might because that the low temperature below 160 °C is not enough to destroy the structure of urea. As the CQDs/TiO2(160–10) samples have the highest dye removal efficiency (92 %), we use these samples for comparison of their performance with pure CQDs and P25. However, only negligible color removal under the same condition was observed in the pure CQDs and P25 groups, indicating that the as-prepared CQDs/TiO2 composites have a notable advantage as photocatalysts over the pure CQDs and P25. The highest photodegradation efficiency of 92 % is achieved during very short heating time (10 min). The irritation of the UV–Vis light on the surface of the CQDs/TiO2 composites promotes the activated CQDs’ electron and holes to excite from valence band to conduction band by the fast generation of holes in the valance band as well as the effective separation of the electron-hole pairs, which has been mentioned in Ref. [31]. Doping with CQDs notably enhanced the visible light absorption compared with the pure P25. Besides the conceivable contributions of CQDs, as discussed above, amino groups on the CQDs/TiO2 surface can be maximumly remained when using the heating temperature of 160 °C during the synthesis, which could be another reason of the highest catalytic efficiency.

The ratio of CQDs and TiO2 (C/T) in the composites also have influence on the photodegradation property. As shown in Fig. 7(b), C/T 0.25 and C/T 1.00 exhibits photocatalytic efficiency of 40.5% and 92.0%, respectively. However, if the C/T was further increased to 1.25:1, the composites show a reduced photocatalytic efficiency of 79%, which is attributed to that excessive CQDs loaded onto the TiO2 surface restricts the absorption of visible light.

The reusability of dried photocatalyst was carried out for the next four cycles in dye photodegradation as shown in Fig. 7(c). It is clearly recorded that the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composites towards degradation of MB dye is more than 80% for the first two cycles. The decolorization rate was found to be decreased after the third cycle. During the photodegradation of MB, anionic ions, such as Cl−, SO3 −, and CO3 2−HCO3 –, would deposit and accumulate on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 surface with the increasing number of times used. Those anions might act as competitive ions as they can generate corresponding radical anions which has lower reactivity compared with hydroxyl radicals, and therefore scavenge available hydroxyl radicals for the photodegradation process [39,40]. Besides, Table 1 summarizes degradation efficiency of other CQDs/Semiconductors for different organic dyes.

The above results of MB photodegradation show that the CQDs/TiO2 has much higher photocatalytic activity compared to the TiO2, which indicates the important role of the CQDs. It is usually considered that photocatalytic ability is closely related to the separation and transfer efficiency of photoinduced carriers in the photocatalyst. Because CQDs possess excellent capability of electron storage, therefore the photo-generated e− from TiO2 could travel through the conduction network of CQDs, so as to inhibit the recombination inhibit the recombination of photo-generated e− and h+ and thus improving the photo-catalytic activity. This can be probed by PL and EIS measurements. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the PL spectra of catalysts were recorded at a wavelength of 300 nm under inactive conditions. The PL spectra of catalysts were recorded at a wavelength of 300 nm under inactive conditions. All samples showed strong and broad emission peaks between 300 and 550 nm as well as two clear excitation peaks at 410 nm and 470 nm, respectively. PL intensity, which is negatively correlated with electron-hole separation efficiency. It can be seen that CQDs/TiO2 displays weaker intensity, indicating the higher electron-hole separation efficiency than TiO2. The results can be attributed to that the excellent charge transfer ability of CQDs allowed CQDs/TiO2 composites to accept photo-generated electrons from TiO2, which made the PL intensity of CQDs/TiO2 lower than that of TiO2. The Nyquist plots of the TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites were tested under the simulated solar light illumination as shown in Fig. 8(b). The prepared CQDs/TiO2 shows a smaller semicircular diameter compare to TiO2 under light illumination, revealing that the CQDs/TiO2 possesses less photo-induced charge-transfer resistance in the interface of the electrode material and electrolyte. The reduced charge-transfer resistance is the important reason for the improved charge separation efficiency and enhanced photo-electron performance. In order to further explore the active substances produced by CQDs/TiO2 composites under light conditions, reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated in the photocatalytic process were detected by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) instrument. It can be seen from the Fig. 8(c–d), no ESR signal was detected for the both two samples in the absence of light, indicating that CQDs/TiO2 or TiO2 could not directly produce free radicals. But when exposed to the stimulated visible light, ESR signals of 1:2:2:1 and 1:1:1:1 were detected, indicating the formation of
OH and
O2 –, respectively. Therefore, it can be proved that
OH and
O2 – were involved in the TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 systems.

CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were successfully prepared by using the TEMPO oxidized cellulose nanofiber as the carbon source. The CQDs/TiO2 samples that were synthesized with a heating temperature of 160 °C and a heating time of 10 min exhibit the highest photocatalytic degradation of MB (92%) within 10 min of treatment, not just under the UV irradiation but the visible light. And more importantly, the bio-based catalyst doesn’t cause the secondary pollution to the wastewater and can be reused for several times. The non-metal doping strategy developed here show significantly improved photocatalytic activity for MB degradation under visible light irradiation. The possible mechanism is rationally discussed as: (1) the recombination rate of photo-induced electron-hole pairs were decreased by CQDs and their surface amino groups; (2) the amino groups on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 are speculated to improve the activity of the photocatalyst probably because of saving photo-generated radicals by assisting the rearrangement of MB. Compared with other strategies, such as hydrothermal method, we believe that our preparation strategy of this photocatalyst is simple, time-saving, low-cost and efficient, which is expected to be used in large scale.
Jingwei Gong: Writing – original draft. Yanzhu Guo: . Jie Lu: Supervision. Yi Cheng: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Haisong Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21978029, 31770624), the Natural Foundation Guidance Program of Liaoning Province (No. 2019-ZD-0219, No. 1553069548324), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC2002024) and the Startup fund for Young Doctor at Dalian Polytechnic University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Present applications of titanium dioxide for the photocatalytic removal of pollutants from water: A review
Environ Manage.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.P. Gopinath, N.V. Madhav, A. Krishnan, et al. Present applications of titanium dioxide for the photocatalytic removal of pollutants from water: A review, Environ Manage. 270 (2020) 110906.

Hazard Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel p–n heterojunction nanocomposite: TiO2 CQDs/ZnBi2O4 photocatalyst with Considerably Enhanced Photocatalytic Activity under Visible-Light Irradiation
Phys. Chem. C.

Dalton Trans.

Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.

ACS Appl. Mater. Inter.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Jin, E. Shaaban, S. Bamonte, et al. Surface Basicity of Metal @ TiO2 to Enhance Photocatalytic Efficiency for CO2 Reduction, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 32 (2021) 38595-38603.
